item.title: Bajaj ransomware, Cordyceps GitHub flaws, Mistic and SharkLoader campaigns
item.content:encoded:
StrikeShark: investigating a new campaign delivering Cobalt Strike through SharkLoader — Must Read

Kaspersky uncovered StrikeShark, a previously undocumented campaign using the new SharkLoader malware to deploy Cobalt Strike against government, diplomatic, software development, and other organizations across multiple countries. The actor appears to gain access opportunistically through public-facing application exploits and malicious droppers, then uses persistence, API-hooking evasion, reconnaissance, and credential theft techniques.

  • Kaspersky uncovered a previously undocumented malware family named SharkLoader during research into activity affecting a diplomatic organization in Indonesia.
  • SharkLoader is used to deploy Cobalt Strike Beacon and was delivered through exploitation of internet-facing applications as well as through custom droppers disguised as legitimate software.
  • Observed exploitation involved products including Microsoft Exchange, Microsoft SharePoint, Openfire Server, GeoServer, Apache Shiro, Hikvision products, Zimbra Collaboration Suite, F5 BIG-IP, Fortinet FortiOS, Cisco IOS XE, and React Server Components.
  • SharkLoader uses DLL side-loading, encrypted components, reflective loading, API hooks, and memory protection changes around Cobalt Strike Beacon execution.
  • Victims identified in the campaign include government-related entities, software development companies, and other organizations in Indonesia, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Lebanon, Syria, Colombia, North Macedonia, Nepal, and Serbia.

Cordyceps CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repositories to Supply-Chain Attacks — Should Read

Researchers identified a CI/CD workflow weakness dubbed Cordyceps that lets unauthenticated GitHub users abuse pull request workflows to run code, steal credentials, and potentially take over repositories. Scans found more than 300 high-impact repositories exposed, including projects tied to Microsoft, Google, Apache, Cloudflare, and the Python Software Foundation.

  • Novee Security identified a CI/CD workflow weakness named Cordyceps that can allow attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains.
  • Novee Security said the flaw is exploitable by any unauthenticated user with only a free account, without organization membership or special privileges.
  • A scan of about 30,000 high-impact repositories found more than 300 to be fully exploitable, enabling attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and supply chain compromise.
  • Novee Security reported examples affecting Microsoft Azure Sentinel, Google adk-samples, Apache Doris, Cloudflare Workers SDK, and Python Software Foundation’s Black project.
  • Following responsible disclosure, Microsoft and Google confirmed impact, while Cloudflare, Python, and Apache applied hardening and patches.

Stealthy Mistic backdoor linked to ransomware access broker KongTuke — Should Read

A new stealthy backdoor called Mistic has been linked to KongTuke, an initial access broker that sells compromised network access to ransomware groups. The malware is designed for long-term persistence, in-memory execution, and low-visibility control of infected systems.

  • Symantec researchers observed a new backdoor called Mistic in financially motivated attacks targeting organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors.
  • Mistic is believed to be linked to KongTuke, also known as Woodgnat, an initial access broker that sells compromised corporate network access to ransomware groups including Qilin, Interlock, Rhysida, Akira, 8Base, and Black Basta.
  • In attacks investigated by Symantec, a legitimate executable named MpExtMs.exe was used to side-load a malicious version.dll that loads Mistic, while a separate .NET DLL displays a fake login screen to steal credentials.
  • Mistic can upload, download, move, rename, and delete files; create folders; change how often it checks in with its command-and-control server; execute code in memory; and delete itself from a host.
  • Zscaler tracks Mistic as MTLBackdoor and reported that it was delivered through a multi-stage ClickFix infection chain in May, with the ability to load Beacon Object Files to expand its capabilities.

Indian auto giant Bajaj Auto hit by ransomware incident — Should Read

Bajaj Auto disclosed a ransomware incident affecting its operations and its technology subsidiary, saying containment and mitigation efforts have so far been successful. The company has not identified the attacker or said whether data was stolen or a ransom was demanded.

  • Bajaj Auto disclosed in a regulatory filing that it was hit by a ransomware attack affecting its operations and Bajaj Auto Technology Limited.
  • The company said it became aware of the incident on Tuesday morning and took precautionary measures to contain its impact.
  • Bajaj Auto said its technical team and cybersecurity experts responded immediately and that mitigation efforts had so far been successful.
  • Bajaj Auto did not disclose the threat actor, whether any data was stolen, or whether the attackers made a ransom demand.
  • The disclosure comes amid other cyber incidents involving major Indian manufacturers, including a recent Tata Electronics incident linked to claims by the World Leaks extortion group.

A Free Gmail Account Impersonating an Internal Employee Asked Payroll to Change a Bank Account — May Read

A payroll redirect BEC used only display-name impersonation from a free Gmail account to pose as an internal employee and request a direct-deposit change, passing SPF, DKIM, and DMARC because there was no malicious payload. The key defense is mandatory out-of-band verification for any banking-change request, regardless of the apparent sender.

  • An attacker used a free Gmail account, churchoffice11011[at]gmail[.]com, with a display name matching a real internal employee at a financial institution to contact a payroll recipient by first name.
  • The email requested an update to the employee’s direct-deposit banking information and contained no links, attachments, or malicious code.
  • SPF, DKIM, and DMARC all passed because the message was legitimately sent through Gmail infrastructure, while the deception was limited to the display name.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 75% confidence as business email compromise and vendor email compromise based on the mismatch between the claimed identity and the unrelated consumer Gmail address.
  • The article recommends out-of-band verification for all direct-deposit, ACH, or wire changes, such as calling the employee at a known pre-existing number before processing the request.

How much cyber risk does AI create for organizations? 457 million security issues. Here’s what you can do about it. — Optional

Tenable says AI tools, especially unapproved “shadow AI,” are creating massive security exposure through misconfigurations, unmanaged dependencies, exposed credentials, and attack paths that traditional CVE-focused vulnerability programs miss. Organizations should move toward continuous, AI-driven exposure management to map risk across assets and remediate the most critical paths faster.

  • Tenable detected 457 million AI-related security issues across more than 7,000 organizations during a 30-day period, averaging 62,000 exposures per organization.
  • The article says AI-related risks are often tied to misconfigurations, unmanaged model dependencies, exposed credentials within AI workloads, and other non-CVE issues rather than standard CVEs.
  • Tenable telemetry cited in the article showed organizations still exposed to older vulnerabilities including CVE-2024-21762 in Fortinet FortiOS, CVE-2021-44228 Log4Shell, and CVE-2017-0144 WannaCry.
  • The article cites the 2026 Verizon DBIR as saying vulnerability exploitation is the most common initial access vector, while most breaches begin with non-CVE issues such as misconfigurations, stolen credentials, or exposed secrets.
  • The article argues that security teams should move beyond legacy vulnerability scanning to AI-driven exposure management that continuously maps attack paths and prioritizes fixes for paths leading to critical assets.

Dawn of the Apex Agentic Adversary — Optional

The article argues that agentic AI will make attacks faster, more targeted, and harder to catalog, especially across converged IT/OT environments. Its main takeaway is that organizations need accurate asset discovery and attack-path visibility to close blind spots before automated adversaries exploit them.

  • The article says frontier agentic models emerging in early 2026 can actively test code and sharply compress the time between vulnerability discovery and weaponization.
  • The article argues that organizations have increased risk by giving AI agents write access to repositories and allowing third-party AI wrappers to connect to internal APIs.
  • The article says AI-driven breaches may become fast, targeted, and mutated enough that traditional public tracking sources such as CISA's KEV Catalog and EPSS may not capture them in time.
  • The article warns that converged IT and OT environments can let automated attackers move from corporate systems into industrial networks through paths such as technician laptops, gateways, and insecure industrial protocols like Modbus, BACnet, and S7comm.
  • The article promotes runZero capabilities for discovering hidden IT, IoT, and OT assets, validating network segmentation assumptions, mapping attack paths, and prioritizing choke points where vulnerabilities intersect with viable paths to critical assets.

item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20230 Exploitation, Huione Seizure, and Bashe’s Fake Hargreaves Lansdown Claim
